selection_reason: Top 3 drifters from reference.json vs latest.json. Only 3 benchmarks show positive drift (regressed): object_creation +49.35%, sort +21.43%, closure_bench +3.70%. All others are flat or improved. No user_priority.md exists.

candidates.hot_function: transform_chain (500K iters), create_and_sum (200K iters), complex_objects (100K iters)

ir_summary.note: Non-inlined profileTier2Func IR for transform_chain. Inlined counts from R35 dump test in extra fields.

disasm_summary.note: Non-inlined transform_chain (988 insns). Inlined: create_and_sum=1181, transform_chain=1572 from R35 dump test.

observations: ROOT CAUSE KNOWN (R35 bisect): commit 39b5ef3 introduced +49% regression via (1) write-only Shape *Shape GC pointer on every Table, (2) ScanGCRoots scanning full vm.regs instead of active frame. Both are correctness fixes (SIGSEGV prevention). [HIGH confidence — R35 git bisect output, opt/knowledge/r35-object-creation-regression.md]

Non-inlined transform_chain IR shows 5 Call instructions per loop iteration (3 GetGlobal + new_vec3 + vec3_add + vec3_scale). With InlineGlobals (production path), callees are inlined: 1572 insns total, 758 memory (48.2%). Memory-dominated because each new_vec3 creates a table + 3 SetField. [HIGH confidence — /tmp/cg_object_creation.log lines 4-43, R35 dump test output]

58% of non-inlined ARM64 instructions are memory operations (573/988). This is consistent with allocation-heavy workload: table creation + field writes dominate. [HIGH confidence — python3 instruction classifier on /tmp/gscript_object_creation_transform_t2.bin]

The regression is GC-driven, not codegen-driven. IR quality and instruction counts match R35 baselines exactly (1181/1572/208 = 0% drift). The ~800K tables created each carry a write-only *Shape GC pointer that was absent at reference time. [HIGH confidence — TestObjectCreationDump output: all 3 functions at +/-0% of baseline]

R35 proposed forward fix: (1) remove dead Table.shape *Shape field (write-only, nothing reads it), (2) restore bounded ScanGCRoots via high-water-mark tracking in vm.regHighWater. Both are surgical changes to runtime, not JIT codegen. [MEDIUM confidence — proposal in opt/knowledge/r35-object-creation-regression.md, not yet implemented or measured]

quicksort IR has 8 blocks with an inner partition loop (B3->B4->B5) doing GetTable comparisons + conditional swap (B4: 2 GetTable + 2 SetTable). Post-loop block B6 does final swap + 2 recursive Call via GetGlobal. [HIGH confidence — /tmp/cg_sort.log lines 4-63]

1602 total ARM64 instructions (6408 bytes) for a 3-param recursive quicksort. 42.6% memory (682), 39.6% compute (635), 17.5% branch (281). The high branch count reflects the partition loop + GetTable exit-resume paths. [HIGH confidence — python3 instruction classifier]

R35 knowledge doc attributes sort regression to the same commit 39b5ef3 that caused object_creation regression. sort uses integer-indexed GetTable/SetTable (not SetField/shapes), so only ScanGCRoots change (uncapped register scan) applies — the Shape pointer is irrelevant for integer-keyed tables. [HIGH confidence — opt/knowledge/r35-object-creation-regression.md Risk Notes section]

The inner loop has untyped Le comparison (v19 = Le v17, v8 : bool) — not LeInt — because GetTable returns `any`. This means runtime type dispatch on every comparison. Sort elements are ints from LCG, so a typed-feedback Le->LeInt specialization could help, but this is codegen improvement not regression fix. [MEDIUM confidence — IR line 28 in /tmp/cg_sort.log]
Live run 0.050s vs reference 0.042s (+19.0%). Consistent with latest.json 0.051s. The +21% drift is real and reproducible, not measurement noise. [HIGH confidence — live benchmark run]

test_accumulator IR is compact: 4 blocks, tight loop B1->B2. Loop body has 1 Call to counter() closure + 1 untyped Add (v13 = Add v22, v11 : any). The Add is untyped because Call returns `any`. [HIGH confidence — /tmp/cg_closure_accumulator.log lines 3-31]

463 total ARM64 instructions, 50.8% memory (235). Small code size reflects simple loop structure. The Call to the closure dominates loop body cost — each invocation exits to Go runtime for closure dispatch. [HIGH confidence — python3 instruction classifier]

Live run 0.027s matches reference exactly (0.027s). The latest.json shows 0.028s (+3.7%) but live measurement shows no regression. This is within measurement noise (1ms on a 27ms benchmark = 3.7%). [HIGH confidence — live run vs reference comparison]

R35 knowledge doc suggests closure_bench regression is from ScanGCRoots change (closure upvalue objects go through gcLog). However, live measurement shows no actual regression — the +3.7% in latest.json may be single-shot noise not present in median runs. [MEDIUM confidence — R35 speculation + contradicted by live run measurement]

bisect_candidates: 39b5ef34 — confirmed culprit for object_creation +49% and likely sort +21% (R35 bisect). Two changes: Table.shape *Shape GC pointer + ScanGCRoots uncapped scan.

cross_candidate_analysis.common_root_cause: Commit 39b5ef3 (Shape system + ScanGCRoots uncapped scan) is the confirmed root cause for object_creation (+49.35%) and highly likely for sort (+21.43%). closure_bench (+3.70%) shows no actual regression in live measurement — noise artifact.
cross_candidate_analysis.recommended_focus: object_creation + sort share same GC-overhead root cause. Single fix addresses both: (1) remove write-only Table.shape *Shape field, (2) restore bounded ScanGCRoots via high-water-mark. These are runtime changes (internal/runtime/table.go + internal/vm/vm.go), not JIT codegen changes.
cross_candidate_analysis.closure_bench_verdict: Below noise threshold in live measurement (0.027s = reference). Not actionable this round.
